segments.text: Uh,
so everybody's here.
Yeah, we all are here,
so we can get started when you're ready.
We have one hour with you.
So if you would like to start by some,
a brief opening remarks.
Yes. And then there will be Christians from members.
Thank you. Perfect. Thank you. Praise.
Thank you. Uh, Dr. Palmier. Uh, good afternoon.
I'm Craig Stobo, chair of the FMA. Uh, thank you very much.
Opportunity to speak to you
and answer any queries you may have.
Uh, it's been a year of progressive, incredibly proud
of our work on locking innovation
through the regulatory sandbox
and helping businesses to operate in new ways.
At the same time, we've worked with the NZX
and MB to make stock exchange listings easier,
while addressing the global shifts towards private markets
with their challenges of valuation and liquidity.
These efforts are all about developing
and building confidence in our financial markets,
ensuring they remain attractive for investors
and businesses alike.
It's a collective achievement that reflects the strength
of our partnerships and the commitment of everyone involved.
With that in mind, I would like to hand over
to our chief executive, Samantha Bures. Samantha.
Hi, Tina. Coto. Kato. It's a pleasure to be here.
I'm joined by Leah Mason, who's our General Counsel
and Director of Evaluation and oversight.
Uh, just a few brief remarks.
The year, um, has seen us to deliver on our mandate
to promote and facilitate fair, efficient,
and transparent financial markets.
In March, we successfully implemented the conduct
of financial institutions regime,
which gives us formal oversight over banks, insurers,
and non-bank deposit takers.
And this is for the first time, uh, in, in New Zealand
that they have been regulated,
that they're now being regulated for their conduct.
Uh, we launched our first financial conduct report in June,
uh, to ensure the industry understood our priorities, uh,
with focusing on making us predictable as a regulator,
we took enforcement action where consumer harm was evident,
pursuing a wide range of cases.
We've taken lead on issues around mortgage fraud
and being part of a broader coalition on fighting scams.
KiwiSaver turned 18 this year
and remains a cornerstone of Kiwi's.
Uh, long-term wealth funds under management have now reached
123 billion.
We very much welcome policy discussion on the
future of Kiwi Saver.
Uh, we note, uh,
and are concerned that just under a third of members
of working age are not contributing at the moment.
Uh, we've begun policy work on custody,
and as Kiwi Saver funds, uh, rise substantially,
it's important to consider the role of the businesses
who actually hold this money,
and they're not directly regulated for that purpose.
Internally, we continue
to focus on the culture and on our people.
This year in review was the first time total FMA staff
numbers fell since the FMA was founded in 2011.
And I acknowledge the work of our staff
during this difficult period.
I'm very proud to report that our gender pay gap, uh,
which also includes my pay now,
sits at half a percent down from almost 10%,
uh, two years ago.
So, our ease of doing business survey is a very useful tool
for understanding stakeholders views many responses were
positive, but also offer some valuable insight on
how we can do better looking ahead.
There's significant policy work on the horizon.
The need for a clear regulatory framework for digital assets
and ongoing work to ensure the future
of New Zealand's capital markets requires ongoing
support and attention.
So, that's it. Thank you very much for your time
and very, very much welcome your questions.
Thank you. We'll start with
Ena Williams. Dear
reporter. And hello to you, Mr. Chair.
All the way in, somewhere sunny.
Um, I, um, look blown away by, um, just
how busy the FMA has been this year.
Um, you've canceled licenses, filed criminal charges,
taken some action against major institutions.
Um, should we be worried that, uh,
firms are treating misconduct as low risk in New Zealand?
Yeah. Look, are they treating misconduct as low risk?
It is very much the focus of our enforcement activity is
to show that whilst we are an engagement led regulator,
there are consequences
and significant consequences for misconduct.
We focus our enforcement action on the most egregious
misconduct cases.
We have achieved some significant wins through the courts,
uh, this, this year.
We have seen our enforcement work, uh, in increase
over the past year,
and that has been a function of the economic times
that we are in, as well as the expansion of our function.
Liam, I dunno if you wanted to add anything from
Yeah. Well, two, two
things.
I think, um, the, I think the vast majority of firms, uh,
will try to do the right thing.
Um, but incentives is what it's all about.
And that's, that's where our supervisory engagement
with the firms is, is that is
where we change behavior most of the time.
Um, what we, what we are seeing as Samantha said, is
an unfortunate, uh, number of cases,
which are simple fraud and scams.
Um, that's by no means anything other than the, you know,
a very small minority of the industry,
but it's a minority that has a big impact.
And so it is important that we keep up the prevention work,
keep up the education work, keep up the enforcement work.
Yeah. And we,
and we're still seeing in some of our fair dealing work,
we're still seeing ongoing lack of investment in systems

of offerings to customers is, is unable to be delivered

And that that's a, you know, that that's an ongoing issue.
That will become an increasing focus for us.

We have a broader range of tools that we can use.
We have thus far, and will until March this year,
only been able to deal

of enforcement, as a matter of breach
of fair dealing requirements,
we now have the broader proactive supervisory toolkit

I could add something else. Uh, Irina, um,
thank you for your question.
We are changing our, uh, way we deliver our regulation,

getting in the boardrooms
of businesses addressing governors, stewards of companies,

and their senior leadership team meetings, to,
to make them aware of what we are interested in,
which is why the financial conduct report is so important.
